b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that if a business accelerates sales and marketing before resolving an upstream supply/fulfillment constraint, it will exacerbate the constraint — meaning the supply bottleneck will arrive sooner than it otherwise would, not be avoided.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Business has an existing supply or fulfillment ceiling
Sales growth is demand-driven and directly loads the constrained facility
b_full.mechanism: Higher sales volume increases throughput demand against a fixed production ceiling, consuming available capacity headroom faster and pulling the constraint forward in time.
bounded_by: Does not apply if the supply constraint has slack capacity sufficient to absorb projected sales growth

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argued that a business operator who is dependent on 3-4 referral partners feels compelled to accept all business those partners send, including low-margin or operationally difficult deals, because their leverage to decline is low when supply is scarce; expanding to 30-40 partners restores the ability to selectively turn down deals.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: referral-partner-dependent business
fewer than 5 active referral partners
b_full.mechanism: With only 3-4 referral sources, losing one partner materially reduces revenue, creating a financial hostage dynamic where the operator accepts unfavorable deals rather than risk partner attrition; expanding the partner base removes this constraint by making any single partner's business non-critical.

numbers.axis: referral partner count vs operator negotiating leverage

anchors.value: low leverage; must accept all business including add-on services that complicate operations
high leverage; can turn down ~25% of business that requires operationally costly add-ons without material revenue loss

conditions: service businesses that have reached capacity on a high-touch premium offer
b_full.mechanism: The new lower-price product requires the same acquisition spend as the high-ticket offer, compressing margins to the point where the business cannot make back customer acquisition costs quickly, producing cash-flow problems.
bounded_by: Applies when the root cause is supply, not demand; if demand were weak, a lower-price product would be the right lever

b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: team or founder troubleshooting growth constraint
b_full.mechanism: Vague labels like 'messaging' cover multiple distinct failure modes (unqualified prospects, wrong offer framing, sales team skill gap) that require different fixes; without precise definition and evidence, effort is allocated to the symptom rather than the cause.

b_full.speaker_term: get the demand, get the cash, then get the resources

a_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Business has established paid lead generation producing more leads than current salespeople can handle

bounded_by: If cash flow does not support hiring, the constraint is financial, not structural, and requires a different decision tree

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi claims that the correct signal for a nail salon model being truly proven is not revenue level but operational constraint: when the business cannot accept new clients because all technicians are fully booked and all chairs occupied, the founder's question should shift from how to get more customers to whether to expand the location or move to virtual — only that capacity-constrained state confirms the model is working.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
b_full.speaker_term: I would know that you had a good model if you said we can't accept new nail customers
conditions: brick-and-mortar nail salon
evaluating model readiness for franchise
b_full.mechanism: Full capacity utilization proves that demand exceeds supply, which validates the offer; the next strategic question becomes supply expansion (new location) or format shift (virtual), not demand generation.
bounded_by: Capacity utilization alone is insufficient if margins are below 30% (model has a separate profitability problem even if fully booked)

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained, founders often build an entirely new lower-price product rather than solving the original supply constraint, which doubles acquisition cost without solving capacity and breaks unit economics because the cheaper product costs the same to acquire as the premium one.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service businesses that have reached capacity on a high-touch premium offer
b_full.mechanism: The new lower-price product requires the same acquisition spend as the high-ticket offer, compressing margins to the point where the business cannot make back customer acquisition costs quickly, producing cash-flow problems.
bounded_by: Applies when the root cause is supply, not demand; if demand were weak, a lower-price product would be the right lever

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi claims that a business owner who is not allocating attention and resources to their highest-return activities is the fundamental reason their business does not grow — and that a new operator taking over and reallocating those same resources typically produces immediate growth from identical inputs.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: small to mid-market service business
owner-operated
b_full.mechanism: Founders develop blind spots to underutilized resources (contacts, followers, goodwill) that a fresh operator sees as obvious; the growth comes from resource reallocation, not resource addition.

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that vague problem statements like 'our messaging is off' must be forced through three diagnostic questions — what does it mean (definition), how do you know (evidence), and why do I care (utility) — before any tactical solution is worth pursuing, because without this forcing function, teams optimize the wrong variable.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: team or founder troubleshooting growth constraint
b_full.mechanism: Vague labels like 'messaging' cover multiple distinct failure modes (unqualified prospects, wrong offer framing, sales team skill gap) that require different fixes; without precise definition and evidence, effort is allocated to the symptom rather than the cause.

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi claims that when a service business is in near-equilibrium between supply and demand — enough demand to stretch capacity but not enough to clearly exceed it — the correct move is to acquire the demand first, collect the cash, and then use that cash to fund the additional supply (staff, training) needed to fulfill it.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
b_full.speaker_term: get the demand, get the cash, then get the resources
conditions: service business near capacity equilibrium
can collect meaningful revenue in first 30 days of a new customer relationship
staff have flex capacity (can do more hours for higher pay)
b_full.mechanism: First-30-day cash collection funds incremental staff costs; because the business collects more in month 1 than it paid to acquire the customer, demand-first scaling is self-financing.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business has no first-30-day revenue (deferred-payment models)
Does not apply when training a new employee takes 6+ months and the cost of errors is unacceptable

bounded_by: Does not apply if the supply constraint has slack capacity sufficient to absorb projected sales growth

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a business shows supply constraint (cannot deliver to existing demand), the correct operational sequence before adding marketing is: (1) raise prices and improve packaging to free cash flow, (2) use that freed cash flow to fund attribution-tracked paid ads, (3) then layer in content as a long-term thought-leadership play; skipping step one and jumping to marketing accelerates failure.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business that is supply-constrained (demand exceeds capacity)
founder asking about marketing/lead generation
b_full.mechanism: A supply-constrained business that adds demand before fixing capacity creates fulfillment failures, which drives churn and reputation damage faster than new customers can offset; pricing first provides the cash to fund the subsequent steps.
bounded_by: Reversed when the business is demand-constrained (supply exceeds demand)
Does not apply if cash flow is already abundant and pricing is already optimized

a_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that commercial recurring clients are more valuable than residential one-time clients for local service businesses because the revenue is recurring on a monthly basis and the contract size is larger, making commercial accounts a higher-LTV customer segment. However, he does not recommend pivoting to commercial as a primary strategy before optimizing existing residential channels.
a_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Local service business (power washing, cleaning)
Existing residential acquisition channels working
a_full.mechanism: Recurring commercial contracts produce predictable monthly revenue versus unpredictable residential one-time transactions, improving revenue stability and LTV.
bounded_by: Pivot to commercial is premature if residential acquisition channels are not yet fully optimized

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argued that a business operator who is dependent on 3-4 referral partners feels compelled to accept all business those partners send, including low-margin or operationally difficult deals, because their leverage to decline is low when supply is scarce; expanding to 30-40 partners restores the ability to selectively turn down deals.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: referral-partner-dependent business
fewer than 5 active referral partners
b_full.mechanism: With only 3-4 referral sources, losing one partner materially reduces revenue, creating a financial hostage dynamic where the operator accepts unfavorable deals rather than risk partner attrition; expanding the partner base removes this constraint by making any single partner's business non-critical.

numbers.axis: referral partner count vs operator negotiating leverage

anchors.value: low leverage; must accept all business including add-on services that complicate operations
high leverage; can turn down ~25% of business that requires operationally costly add-ons without material revenue loss

Changing demand-side mechanics while supply is the binding constraint wastes resources and does not remove the actual growth ceiling.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Business has more demand than it can fulfill
Founder is the sole delivery resource
b_full.mechanism: If sales are capped by delivery capacity, adding demand only deepens the backlog; only expanding or restructuring delivery capacity removes the ceiling.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business is demand-constrained (not enough customers)

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained, founders often build an entirely new lower-price product rather than solving the original supply constraint, which doubles acquisition cost without solving capacity and breaks unit economics because the cheaper product costs the same to acquire as the premium one.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service businesses that have reached capacity on a high-touch premium offer
b_full.mechanism: The new lower-price product requires the same acquisition spend as the high-ticket offer, compressing margins to the point where the business cannot make back customer acquisition costs quickly, producing cash-flow problems.
bounded_by: Applies when the root cause is supply, not demand; if demand were weak, a lower-price product would be the right lever

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that vague problem statements like 'our messaging is off' must be forced through three diagnostic questions — what does it mean (definition), how do you know (evidence), and why do I care (utility) — before any tactical solution is worth pursuing, because without this forcing function, teams optimize the wrong variable.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: team or founder troubleshooting growth constraint
b_full.mechanism: Vague labels like 'messaging' cover multiple distinct failure modes (unqualified prospects, wrong offer framing, sales team skill gap) that require different fixes; without precise definition and evidence, effort is allocated to the symptom rather than the cause.

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi claims that when a service business is in near-equilibrium between supply and demand — enough demand to stretch capacity but not enough to clearly exceed it — the correct move is to acquire the demand first, collect the cash, and then use that cash to fund the additional supply (staff, training) needed to fulfill it.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
b_full.speaker_term: get the demand, get the cash, then get the resources
conditions: service business near capacity equilibrium
can collect meaningful revenue in first 30 days of a new customer relationship
staff have flex capacity (can do more hours for higher pay)
b_full.mechanism: First-30-day cash collection funds incremental staff costs; because the business collects more in month 1 than it paid to acquire the customer, demand-first scaling is self-financing.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business has no first-30-day revenue (deferred-payment models)
Does not apply when training a new employee takes 6+ months and the cost of errors is unacceptable

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a business shows supply constraint (cannot deliver to existing demand), the correct operational sequence before adding marketing is: (1) raise prices and improve packaging to free cash flow, (2) use that freed cash flow to fund attribution-tracked paid ads, (3) then layer in content as a long-term thought-leadership play; skipping step one and jumping to marketing accelerates failure.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi

b_full.mechanism: A supply-constrained business that adds demand before fixing capacity creates fulfillment failures, which drives churn and reputation damage faster than new customers can offset; pricing first provides the cash to fund the subsequent steps.
bounded_by: Reversed when the business is demand-constrained (supply exceeds demand)
Does not apply if cash flow is already abundant and pricing is already optimized

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argued that a business operator who is dependent on 3-4 referral partners feels compelled to accept all business those partners send, including low-margin or operationally difficult deals, because their leverage to decline is low when supply is scarce; expanding to 30-40 partners restor

a_full.text: Alex Hormozi argued that a business operator who is dependent on 3-4 referral partners feels compelled to accept all business those partners send, including low-margin or operationally difficult deals, because their leverage to decline is low when supply is scarce; expanding to 30-40 partners restores the ability to selectively turn down deals.
a_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: referral-partner-dependent business
fewer than 5 active referral partners
a_full.mechanism: With only 3-4 referral sources, losing one partner materially reduces revenue, creating a financial hostage dynamic where the operator accepts unfavorable deals rather than risk partner attrition; expanding the partner base removes this constraint by making any single partner's business non-critical.

numbers.axis: referral partner count vs operator negotiating leverage

anchors.value: low leverage; must accept all business including add-on services that complicate operations
high leverage; can turn down ~25% of business that requires operationally costly add-ons without material revenue loss

b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
b_full.speaker_term: I would know that you had a good model if you said we can't accept new nail customers
conditions: brick-and-mortar nail salon

a_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained rather than demand-constrained, changing the selling model is the wrong lever — the delivery model is the correct constraint to address first.
a_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business with a single delivery person or limited delivery capacity
Organic demand already exists and the founder cannot take more customers
a_full.mechanism: Modifying the demand side (e.g., switching to evergreen enrollment) increases inbound pressure on a system that is already at capacity, worsening the constraint rather than solving it.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business is demand-constrained (no inbound leads)

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained, founders often build an entirely new lower-price product rather than solving the original supply constraint, which doubles acquisition cost without solving capacity and breaks unit economics because the cheaper product costs the same to acquire as the premium one.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service businesses that have reached capacity on a high-touch premium offer
b_full.mechanism: The new lower-price product requires the same acquisition spend as the high-ticket offer, compressing margins to the point where the business cannot make back customer acquisition costs quickly, producing cash-flow problems.
bounded_by: Applies when the root cause is supply, not demand; if demand were weak, a lower-price product would be the right lever

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi claims that the correct signal for a nail salon model being truly proven is not revenue level but operational constraint: when the business cannot accept new clients because all technicians are fully booked and all chairs occupied, the founder's question should shift from how to get more customers to whether to expand the location or move to virtual — only that capacity-constrained state confirms the model is working.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
b_full.speaker_term: I would know that you had a good model if you said we can't accept new nail customers
conditions: brick-and-mortar nail salon
evaluating model readiness for franchise
b_full.mechanism: Full capacity utilization proves that demand exceeds supply, which validates the offer; the next strategic question becomes supply expansion (new location) or format shift (virtual), not demand generation.
bounded_by: Capacity utilization alone is insufficient if margins are below 30% (model has a separate profitability problem even if fully booked)

Changing demand-side mechanics while supply is the binding constraint wastes resources and does not remove the actual growth ceiling.

conditions: Business has more demand than it can fulfill
Founder is the sole delivery resource
b_full.mechanism: If sales are capped by delivery capacity, adding demand only deepens the backlog; only expanding or restructuring delivery capacity removes the ceiling.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business is demand-constrained (not enough customers)

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained, founders often build an entirely new lower-price product rather than solving the original supply constraint, which doubles acquisition cost without solving capacity and breaks unit economics because the cheaper product costs the same to acquire as the premium one.

conditions: service businesses that have reached capacity on a high-touch premium offer
b_full.mechanism: The new lower-price product requires the same acquisition spend as the high-ticket offer, compressing margins to the point where the business cannot make back customer acquisition costs quickly, producing cash-flow problems.
bounded_by: Applies when the root cause is supply, not demand; if demand were weak, a lower-price product would be the right lever

conditions: small to mid-market service business
owner-operated
b_full.mechanism: Founders develop blind spots to underutilized resources (contacts, followers, goodwill) that a fresh operator sees as obvious; the growth comes from resource reallocation, not resource addition.

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that vague problem statements like 'our messaging is off' must be forced through three diagnostic questions — what does it mean (definition), how do you know (evidence), and why do I care (utility) — before any tactical solution is worth pursuing, because without this forcing function, teams optimize the wrong variable.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: team or founder troubleshooting growth constraint
b_full.mechanism: Vague labels like 'messaging' cover multiple distinct failure modes (unqualified prospects, wrong offer framing, sales team skill gap) that require different fixes; without precise definition and evidence, effort is allocated to the symptom rather than the cause.

b_full.speaker_term: get the demand, get the cash, then get the resources
conditions: service business near capacity equilibrium
can collect meaningful revenue in first 30 days of a new customer relationship
staff have flex capacity (can do more hours for higher pay)
b_full.mechanism: First-30-day cash collection funds incremental staff costs; because the business collects more in month 1 than it paid to acquire the customer, demand-first scaling is self-financing.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business has no first-30-day revenue (deferred-payment models)
Does not apply when training a new employee takes 6+ months and the cost of errors is unacceptable

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a business shows supply constraint (cannot deliver to existing demand), the correct operational sequence before adding marketing is: (1) raise prices and improve packaging to free cash flow, (2) use that freed cash flow to fund attribution-tracked paid ads, (3) then layer in content as a long-term thought-leadership play; skipping step one and jumping to marketing accelerates failure.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business that is supply-constrained (demand exceeds capacity)
founder asking about marketing/lead generation
b_full.mechanism: A supply-constrained business that adds demand before fixing capacity creates fulfillment failures, which drives churn and reputation damage faster than new customers can offset; pricing first provides the cash to fund the subsequent steps.
bounded_by: Reversed when the business is demand-constrained (supply exceeds demand)
Does not apply if cash flow is already abundant and pricing is already optimized

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a business shows supply constraint (cannot deliver to existing demand), the correct operational sequence before adding marketing is: (1) raise prices and improve packaging to free cash flow, (2) use that freed cash flow to fund attribution-tracked paid ads, (3) then layer in content as a long-term thought-leadership play; skipping step one and jumping to marketing accelerates failure.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business that is supply-constrained (demand exceeds capacity)
founder asking about marketing/lead generation
b_full.mechanism: A supply-constrained business that adds demand before fixing capacity creates fulfillment failures, which drives churn and reputation damage faster than new customers can offset; pricing first provides the cash to fund the subsequent steps.
bounded_by: Reversed when the business is demand-constrained (supply exceeds demand)
Does not apply if cash flow is already abundant and pricing is already optimized

a_full.text: Alex Hormozi claims that the correct signal for a nail salon model being truly proven is not revenue level but operational constraint: when the business cannot accept new clients because all technicians are fully booked and all chairs occupied, the founder's question should shift from how to get more customers to whether to expand the location or move to virtual — only that capacity-constrained state confirms the model is working.
a_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
a_full.speaker_term: I would know that you had a good model if you said we can't accept new nail customers
conditions: brick-and-mortar nail salon
evaluating model readiness for franchise

bounded_by: Capacity utilization alone is insufficient if margins are below 30% (model has a separate profitability problem even if fully booked)

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained, founders often build an entirely new lower-price product rather than solving the original supply constraint, which doubles acquisition cost without solving capacity and breaks unit economics because the cheaper product costs the same to acquire as the premium one.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service businesses that have reached capacity on a high-touch premium offer
b_full.mechanism: The new lower-price product requires the same acquisition spend as the high-ticket offer, compressing margins to the point where the business cannot make back customer acquisition costs quickly, producing cash-flow problems.
bounded_by: Applies when the root cause is supply, not demand; if demand were weak, a lower-price product would be the right lever

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service business is supply-constrained, founders often build an entirely new lower-price product rather than solving the original supply constraint, which doubles acquisition cost without solving capacity and breaks unit economics because the cheaper product costs the same to acquire as the premium one.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service businesses that have reached capacity on a high-touch premium offer

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi claims that when a service business is in near-equilibrium between supply and demand — enough demand to stretch capacity but not enough to clearly exceed it — the correct move is to acquire the demand first, collect the cash, and then use that cash to fund the additional supply (staff, training) needed to fulfill it.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
b_full.speaker_term: get the demand, get the cash, then get the resources
conditions: service business near capacity equilibrium
can collect meaningful revenue in first 30 days of a new customer relationship
staff have flex capacity (can do more hours for higher pay)
b_full.mechanism: First-30-day cash collection funds incremental staff costs; because the business collects more in month 1 than it paid to acquire the customer, demand-first scaling is self-financing.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business has no first-30-day revenue (deferred-payment models)
Does not apply when training a new employee takes 6+ months and the cost of errors is unacceptable

bounded_by: Does not apply when the business has no first-30-day revenue (deferred-payment models)

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a business shows supply constraint (cannot deliver to existing demand), the correct operational sequence before adding marketing is: (1) raise prices and improve packaging to free cash flow, (2) use that freed cash flow to fund attribution-tracked paid ads, (3) then layer in content as a long-term thought-leadership play; skipping step one and jumping to marketing accelerates failure.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business that is supply-constrained (demand exceeds capacity)
founder asking about marketing/lead generation
b_full.mechanism: A supply-constrained business that adds demand before fixing capacity creates fulfillment failures, which drives churn and reputation damage faster than new customers can offset; pricing first provides the cash to fund the subsequent steps.
bounded_by: Reversed when the business is demand-constrained (supply exceeds demand)
Does not apply if cash flow is already abundant and pricing is already optimized

Does not apply if cash flow is already abundant and pricing is already optimized

a_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that at a business generating ~$400K in profit (with owner compensation included), the correct diagnosis is different from a $700K+ profit business: at $400K, the business cannot yet afford to hire a replacement and must first fix underlying business issues before making the key hire. At $700K+, the business is financially ready to hire and the constraint is purely time.
a_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Single-location professional-services business
Owner is the primary licensed delivery provider
a_full.mechanism: Profit level determines whether the business can absorb the cost of a peer-hire replacement; below ~$400K combined earnings the margin is too thin to fund the hire and business fixes must come first.

numbers.axis: Owner total earnings threshold for hire-readiness

anchors.region: too thin to hire peer replacement

bounded_by: Specific to single-location professional-services businesses where owner is the clinical provider

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi advises founders in the $1M-$3M revenue range (the swamp) to do significantly more volume on whatever acquisition channel is currently working before opening new channels, because this stage is typically cash-constrained and the fastest path to clearing the swamp is squeezing the existing channel rather than diversifying.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: $1M-$3M revenue
single working acquisition channel already exists
b_full.mechanism: At this revenue stage, cash flow is insufficient to fund new-channel experimentation; more volume on the proven channel generates the cash surplus needed to later fund diversification.

numbers.axis: revenue range where swamp constraint applies

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a service business in the $1-$3M revenue range (the swamp) faces a structural trap: the founder is overworked, but hiring the first significant employee costs roughly 50% of profit, making the risk feel untenable; this trap does not resolve through marketing but through pricing improvement that widens the margin enough to fund the first hire.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business at $1M-$3M revenue
founder is primary delivery mechanism
b_full.mechanism: At thin margins (~20-25%) a single executive hire consumes too large a share of profit to feel safe; the business cannot grow without the hire, but the hire cannot be funded without prior margin expansion through pricing.

numbers.axis: revenue range vs. founder constraint type

anchors.value: founder doing everything; no cash for hires
first significant hire costs ~50% of profit; trapped
hire cash available; can graduate to who game

bounded_by: Does not apply if gross margins are above 60%; at that level the first hire is proportionally cheaper

Redirecting that salary to a hired physician means the hire effectively costs the business nothing in incremental c
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi estimates that if a founder in a high-margin services business allocates 25% of their time to training and developing their sales team — forgoing roughly $1M in personal annual billings — the disproportionate return from getting each of the other agents even one additional deal per year

a_full.text: Alex Hormozi reframes the hiring decision for a peer clinician as a capital allocation with no net cost: the owner is already paying themselves approximately $200K as medical director. Redirecting that salary to a hired physician means the hire effectively costs the business nothing in incremental cash, while returning 80% of the owner's time for business scaling activities.
a_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
a_full.speaker_term: using your $200,000 salary to give to them
conditions: Owner is taking a medical director draw approximately equal to market rate for a replacement physician
Business has sufficient retained profit to fund operations without the owner's salary redirected
a_full.mechanism: The existing medical director salary is already an operational cost; substituting a hired physician at that same rate converts an unproductive cost (founder doing clinical work that blocks scaling) into a productive one (freed founder time for growth).

numbers.axis: Net incremental cost of peer physician hire

bounded_by: Requires the owner's market-rate draw to approximately equal replacement hire cost
Does not apply if replacement physician market rate significantly exceeds owner's current draw

b_full.text: Alex Hormozi estimates that if a founder in a high-margin services business allocates 25% of their time to training and developing their sales team — forgoing roughly $1M in personal annual billings — the disproportionate return from getting each of the other agents even one additional deal per year will exceed the $1M foregone income.
b_full.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: founder currently producing $4M personally
team of 7+ agents each capable of incremental improvement
high-margin services where each deal is significant
b_full.mechanism: Training leverage: if 7 agents each close one incremental deal annually due to founder coaching, the aggregate additional revenue from those deals exceeds the $1M opportunity cost of the founder's 25% time block.

numbers.axis: Founder time allocation tradeoff at $4M personal production

anchors.region: Cost of 25% time shift to training
Benefit: each of 7 agents closes 1 extra deal/year

bounded_by: Assumes deal size is sufficient that 7 incremental deals exceed $1M total; specific to high-ticket professional services
